prompt: What is the importance of "coherence" in open platform development?
response: I think you do need coherence to some degree because otherwise, you get background noise, which is diffuse. It's entropy. And so you need something that gives a bias so that it accumulates into something more than background noise. And that's so that requires some kind of coherence of like multiple agents pointing in roughly ish the same direction, at least some pockets. So I would argue that it's way easier to find. Coordinating and getting 18 or so different actors to all agree on a direction from a standstill is basically impossible. It's really, really hard. However, if you see that a couple of them are already pointing in roughly the right direction and they could say, hey, we should like work together. It's like, oh, okay. Like that becomes easy. And, you know, you can tweak and go instead of going default divergent, you go like this. So now you're default convergent. Okay. And so now this gets momentum. And now other people nearby who may be at the beginning, I'm not really 100% sure that maybe they were oriented like this. As this gets momentum, it pulls people's incentives in just a little bit. And so now there's people that before were on the margin in a way. Now they're on the margin in, they come in. This creates the pull even stronger. And over time, this gravity wall dynamic can pull in more people once it gets a bunch of momentum. And people go, their opinion of what they want to do individually is overwhelmed by whatever. I'll just go and do the thing that everybody else is doing because it's better to do something with momentum. So you can get this coherence. But it's quite hard to do from a standstill. And I do think that you do need local pockets. If everyone's doing their own individual thing that never adds up to anything more, you don't get anything. You just get noise.

prompt: Can you explain what is meant by "kayfabe" in organizations?
response: Kayfabe is this notion from professional wrestling. And what it means is a thing that everybody knows is fake, that everybody acts like is real. And I think kayfabe is a useful lens for understanding what actually happens in organizations. And organizations, kayfabe is also a little bit of a lubricant in organizations, in the same way that politeness is actually, a lot of politeness is a lot of crushed up little white lies, kind of, but they lubricate interactions in society. So like a little bit of kayfabe is good. It helps make sure everybody is like, yeah, we're going to be bold or whatever. What can happen in organizations in general, and this happens, I think, inexorably to some degree in all organizations over time, is the kayfabe starts off grounded, just a little bit off the ground truth. But then you add more layers and then you add more pressure for big, bold bets. And that means it's harder for people to raise disconfirming evidence up the chain to their manager and say, I don't think that's possible. And so you now have like, they're just playing the game. So now you have something that's decoherent. At a certain point, the kayfabe delaminates from underlying reality and becomes free floating. And this is a very dangerous situation because you now have a thing that looks like it's going in a coherent direction. It is not.

prompt: How does "trust" function in organizations, according to the text?
response: Trust makes it all work. And especially when you've hired very good people, it's going to be like, you have to, when you love it, let it go. Kind of thing you know, so you got to give like people will know and do things that you don't fully understand and they won't be able to represent to you why they are a good idea and sometimes it's like literally information they cannot share with you um because of the power dynamics and and you know what have you or it wouldn't make sense you know or take or take forever to explain and um so it's just a lot of like hire good people, give them quite a bit of leeway, and help ensure that they can think long term. You can think of trust as the lubricant of um of an organization. And it's the thing that allows the organization to navigate ambiguity and to get stronger from it, not weaker. And by default, ambiguity destroys an organization in a low-trust environment. And every little bit of thing is like, oh, I think that person is just trying to be lazy or like do this thing for their own personal gain. Like, great. I mean, you're screwed at that point. Like there's nothing. You're now able to descend into this finger-pointing kind of thing.

prompt: What is "Goodhart's Law" and its implications for metrics in organizations or platforms?
response: Goodhart's law is this notion that once you start optimizing for a metric, it ceases to measure what you care about. And their intuition for this is that the interest of the individual members of the swarm is different than the interest of the collective of the overall total goal. So if you, there's different ways to make good hearts law, it runs, it's, it's inevitable. But there's different ways to make it run faster or slower. So one of the ways that makes it run faster is if you make it so all that matters is this metric. Nothing else matters. This is the only legible signal in the entire system. Then you're going to get all kinds of fraud. Yes, if there's no real names in the thing, there's no connection to real, you're going to get a whole bunch of fraud. And the second thing that happens is how clever are the participants? If they're really clever, then Goodhart's Law will run faster. Because they'll figure out all kinds of weird little loopholes and one weird tricks and whatever. And they will discover an innovation which only innovates the metric, not the underlying reality, which is its own form of kayfabe.

prompt: Can you explain the concept of a "North Star" in organizational or platform development?
response: North Stars can be very powerful clarifying concepts within an organization, within an ecosystem, within a platform, having a very clear, a plausible and inspiring North Star, that other people, that everybody knows about and everybody shares. And that can lead to very coherent outcomes. And creating such a North Star is quite difficult. They're often very low resolution. They must be because you can't control the details of it. You can only control the grand sweep of the thing or give more of the path that it might take on the grand sweep of things. And I think that's very confusing to people because it's very, everyone's used to control in the small as opposed to like this kind of like sweeping arc. In the bigger picture.

prompt: What is the "tragedy of the commons" and how does it apply to micro-entrepreneurial organizations?
response: To the extent there is a shared resource, a resource in common. There is always an, it's a literal tragedy of the commons kind of situation. And one of the shared resources that happens across companies is brand. So, to the extent the brand means something, it is a very, very valuable resource that is also quicker to erode than to build. And so one team can say effectively, they won't ever think they're doing this, but they can say, oh, I can capitalize on the amount of trust we have accrued in that brand to go really fast on this thing in a way that actually will burn trust overall on net, but it'll make my project look really good. And they don't think of it that way, of course, but like that is an underlying dynamic that happened. And that's one of the reasons it's very hard to challenge it.

prompt: What is an adjacency map?
response:
A powerful technique for navigating complex problem domains: adjacency maps.

In these kinds of domains, it’s unclear where to get started.

There are hundreds of possible toeholds, in a big swirling chaotic soup of possibility.

It’s unclear which toeholds will turn out to be viable.

A number of toeholds have obvious adjacencies to grow into if they turn out to be viable.

Which toehold to start with is not just its own value, but the indirect value of the adjacent things to grow into.

That’s where adjacency maps are useful.

Think of them like a tech tree from the game Civilization.

prompt: What are meta-ecosystems?
response:
There are ecosystems (a single platform) and meta-ecosystems (multiple platforms that are all affiliated and act, in some ways, like one).

The difference between them is how many different entities are making coherent decisions about what behavior to ship.

prompt: What is "the quality that cannot be named" as described by Christopher Alexander?
response:
The quality that cannot be named is a concept from Christopher Alexander, for things that have a resonant wholeness to them.

You might call this sublime beauty.

prompt: What does 'epsilon' mean in the context of differential privacy?
response:
Epsilon is a concept from differential privacy.

The epsilon says how likely two distinct datasets that differ by one record could be distinguished (revealing the presence or absence of that record).

A smaller epsilon is more private.

If the epsilon is sufficiently tight, your data is hiding in a tornado of other data, and its presence can't trace back to you.

prompt: What is exaptation?
response: Exaptation: use an existing thing for a novel, unexpected purpose.

prompt: What is the curse of knowledge?
response:
The curse of knowledge.

Knowledge is power.

Power we take for granted.

What's obvious to you is not necessarily obvious to everyone else!

prompt: What is the superpower of LLMs?
response: LLMs’ superpower is translation, from anything to anything.

prompt: How does society capture and preserve useful ideas?
response: Society captures useful ideas as cached answers. If the idea is useful, it’s more likely to be maintained or copied. Or written down or transformed into a more durable form. Ideas that are not useful are not maintained; they evaporate away.

prompt: How do LLMs relate to society's cached answers?
response: LLMs then capture all of those useful ideas that someone bothered to write down, embeds them into a crystallized mirror that can be used to reflect society’s cached answers.

prompt: How do human creators approach creative tasks compared to LLMs?
response: Human creators do a thing not unlike LLM synthesis in creative tasks, but with the addition of taste. Good designers absorb lots of good examples from the web of examples they're exposed to, selecting the subset they like (applying their taste) and then synthesizing that intuition into an answer to a given problem in front of them. Not unlike what LLMs are doing. Though LLMs don't have a taste criteria for what to absorb from.

prompt: Why do LLMs make boring mistakes?
response: LLMs today make boring mistakes because they can’t learn. That is, the model’s weights are fixed at training time.

prompt: Why is unsupervised automation with LLMs difficult?
response: This is one of the reasons that unsupervised automation is tough with LLMs. Even if you get it to work well 95% of the time, that last 5% of reliability is extremely hard to achieve. If there's a human in the loop it doesn't have to be 100% reliable, the human can figure out when it works and bridge the gap. If there’s no human in the loop, there’s no way to absorb the failure cases, and the viability of the whole system is reduced.

prompt: How are individuals using LLMs more effectively than organizations?
response: Individuals are using LLMs way more effectively than organizations today. Individuals are using LLMs in situated contexts, informally and as augmentation to their work, not automation for it. When an individual uses LLMs, there’s a human in the loop every time. The human in the loop can handle failure cases, and steer it more effectively.

prompt: What is the effect of LLMs recommending the most popular answers?
response: LLMs recommending the most popular answers (e.g. 'Just use tailwind.css') will lock them into place for longer and more deeply. Society caches answers already, but now the cache will be locked into place more deeply, and will be harder to change. The most popular answers will get ever-more popular. The less popular answers will have a hard time breaking in. The popular answers will have more staying power. We’ll see more hysteresis in the system.

prompt: How do LLMs behave as statistical summarizers?
response: LLMs are inherently statistical summarizers. Which is why they pull to the centroid. 'What is the most average answer, conditioned on the input so far?'

prompt: What is the impact of the same-origin paradigm on software?
response: The same-origin paradigm leads to software that is too chunky. That is, it’s bigger than ideal for a given piece of functionality. Each app has to bundle within it a lot of stuff to make its pocket universe viable. This chunkiness can run amok with things like aggregation, leading to apps that are like gravity wells. This also means that any given conceivable app is less likely to be viable. Any single component that is not viable makes the whole assemblage not viable. Apps that are larger have more components, and are thus more likely to have at least one component that is not viable, and thus also not be viable. You have to build further in the dark before knowing if your assemblage will be viable. Smaller chunks make building an assemblage cheaper, which leads to more experimentation and discovery of good ideas.

prompt: What is the benefit of reducing the effort needed to create an experience?
response: If the amount of effort to create an experience is very small then you can cover tons and tons of niches. How much work a thing is to write defines how much it has to be generalized to get enough market to make it viable. If it's a small amount of work, it doesn't have to be generalized much to have enough of a market to make it worthwhile.

prompt: What pattern involves using LLMs to astroturf content in an ecosystem?
response: An interesting pattern: using LLMs to astroturf content in an ecosystem. The challenge of an ecosystem is not so much the hill climbing of quality, it's the creation of the ecosystem to a critical mass. And now you can astroturf it!

prompt: How can large-scale systems use LLMs to improve response times?
response: A successful large-scale system will use LLMs to create a lot of answers, and then cache them so in the future a similar question can be answered with a retrieval, not a generation. Guess at the kinds of questions users will have, then unleash a swarm of LLMs on them in a precomputation step. By the time the user asks their question, there’s already a pre-cached answer ready to go. The power of LLMs, but with faster results.

prompt: What is the difference between optimization and creation?
response: Optimization can’t create, it can just curate and sharpen.

prompt: How does capitalism perform across Maslow's hierarchy of needs?
response: Capitalism is great at the lower layers of Maslow's hierarchy but terrible for the higher layers, where optimization destroys meaning. But we use it for both!

prompt: How does misinformation succeed according to the author?
response: Misinformation succeeds not in making you believe a falsehood, but in making you not believe anything at all. 'Everything is corrupt so you can’t trust anything,' it says, as it is the one corrupting things. The side that wants more entropy has an unfair advantage. It’s easy to tear things down, an order of magnitude easier.

prompt: Explain the concept of 'Slime Mold' as it relates to organizational dynamics.
response:
An approachable presentation, done in an emoji style, showing why even when everyone is competent and collaborative, you can still get hurricane-force headwinds.

I've developed various versions of the argument in various contexts and forms over the years. The ideas started when I was studying the emergent power dynamics of Wikipedia's user community in undergrad, and were also informed by participating in web standards. I believe they are not specific to any one context, but rather emerge inherently any time there are individuals with autonomy who care deeply. Instead of ignoring these dynamics, I think it's important to acknowledge and embrace them with a sense of compassion and openness.

prompt: What is meant by 'Gardening Platforms'?
response: A ~200 slide flip book style presentation about how to garden platforms, including the fundamental emergent power dynamics of platforms, how to evolve an existing platform to continuously improve it, and how to create a new platform from scratch.

prompt: What does 'The Iterative Adjacent Possible' refer to?
response: An essay about an alternative way of approaching large problems that leads to much better outcomes with much lower risk.

prompt: What is 'The Live Oak Playbook'?
response: Describes a playbook for resilient and enduring growth in harsh environments.

prompt: Explain the concept of 'The Sarumans and the Radagasts'.
response: Describes two archetypes of deep magic that make the world go round.

prompt: What is 'The Magic of Acorns'?
response: A short strategy parable about the magic of finding and nurturing acorns.

prompt: What is https://thecompendium.cards?
response: https://thecompendium.cards is a place for me to share developing, inter-linked nuggets of insight in public in an incremental way — think of it as my Medium drafts folder, organized as virtual, inter-linked index cards of developing ideas.

prompt: What is the @CardsCompendium Twitter Bot?
response: The @CardsCompendium is a twitter bot that tweets the titles and links of a few cards a day, prioritizing new and popular cards. It’s the easiest way to stay in the loop without getting overwhelmed by all of the content. Many cards will make sense purely from the tweet text, but for those that don’t the link is a great starting point to dig in deeper. Retweets and favorites of the tweets will also show up as stars on the cards in the webapp.

prompt: Why is signing in to the compendium web app recommended?
response: Most of the tools to consume the compendium (described below) save some state to help you reorient yourself. You can use them right away without signing in, and they’ll persist on the device you’re currently using. However, if you plan to consume the compendium on multiple devices, it’s a good idea to sign in with your Google account (it’s just a few taps). All of the state you’ve built up so far will be synced with your account.

prompt: What is the 'read state' of a card in the compendium?
response: Each card is by default unread until you mark it as read. This read state helps you keep track of which cards you’ve already seen, including by making the links to those cards be a different color. If you’ve looked at a card for more than three seconds, it’s marked as read automatically. This read state is automatically synced across devices if you sign in.

prompt: How does the preview card feature work?
response: If you hover over any link to a card or card thumbnail, a preview of the card will show up after a couple of seconds.

prompt: What is the reading list feature in the compendium?
response: When you’re reading a card, you might want to dig into the other cards it links to — but not want to lose your train of thought from the other cards in the collection you’re currently looking at. The reading list is a feature that allows you to add cards to your queue to read next. Think of it like the pattern of opening a link in a new tab as a queue of things to read next, but optimized for the compendium.

prompt: How does starring cards work?
response: You can star cards you love to make it easy to find your favorites — and also to signal to others which cards are worth checking out. A tally of all of the stars shows on the cards (without revealing who starred them).

prompt: How does discussion work on a card?
response: If you’re signed in, you can also start a public comment thread on a particular card or reply to an existing comment thread.

prompt: How does the find feature work in the compendium?
response: If you’re looking for a particular card, you can use the find tool. You can press Ctrl/Cmd-F, or tap the search icon below any card. As you type in the find dialog, cards that contain text that matches are shown.

prompt: How does the compendium behave on mobile devices?
response: If you read the compendium on a device with a small screen, you’ll get a view that emphasizes just the card itself. You can mark cards as read, star them, and add to your reading list, and if you’re signed in they’ll sync to other devices. Currently you can’t view the entire collection of cards at once or navigate to a different collection, but that will be added in the future.

prompt: What is the purpose of tag collections in the compendium?
response: Many cards are tagged with subjects they’re about. Tags show up beneath the card they apply to. If you tap a tag, it will jump to viewing the card you’re looking at, in context of the collection of other cards with that tag.

prompt: What is prompt injection and why is it a security concern?
response:
A security model that relies on LLMs to make security judgments on potentially untrusted data is fundamentally unsound.

- You can’t use LLMs to avoid prompt injection.

- If you say "use an LLM to make a security decision" you're already hosed... no matter how many layers you nest it.

prompt: What does it mean for the same origin policy to be a historical accident?
response:
The same origin policy is not a natural law. It is a *human* law. It was a historical accident!

- I learned this week that apparently it wasn’t even well considered.
- It was a hotfix the Netscape team decided on one night to handle an early Javascript security error.
- Apparently Tim Berners Lee doesn’t like the policy.

It started as a quick fix, and then because it was a reasonable simplifying policy it grew and grew in momentum and import until now it feels like a law of gravity. But unlike a law of gravity, it can be changed. We made it, we can change it.

prompt: How does Goodhart's law apply to alignment in AI or swarms of agents?
response:
Goodhart's law is a form of ‘cheating’.

- Cheating happens with agents who aren’t aligned with the collective as an end in and of itself.
- That means if there’s an action that will get them as an individual an edge at the cost of the collective, they’ll take it.
- You can get strong alignments by having a deeply and widely believed end.

prompt: What is coactive software?
response:
Coactive software means the human and the LLM see the same data and can use the same tools.

- The system and the human can work together, understand each other, correct and extend each other’s work.
- No secrets.
- Clear alignment.
- If it can write code within the fabric you’d have self-adaptive software.

prompt: What is meant by a persistent prompt injection?
response:
A prompt injection stored in your context is a persistent prompt injection.

- Prompt injection attacks that can embed themselves in your personal stored context might never be found.

prompt: What are the consequences of having chatbots as a central UI paradigm?
response: The chatbot paradigm implies a central omniscient single personality. Such a thing is impossible to create the right personality for every moment. It might also be something that subtly manipulates you, since it controls the whole system. Much better to have the chat be a feature that you can call up on demand, with as many different sub personalities as you want.

prompt: Can you explain what is meant by "the fundamental idea of a platform"?
response: That's the fundamental idea of a platform. You just lift people up so that they have less to reach to unlock interesting use cases.

prompt: What does it mean for a platform and ecosystem to "co-evolve"?
response: So the ecosystem in some way is alive in some very meaningful sense, which means that your platform also has to be alive to some degree to evolve, co-evolve with the underlying ecosystem. What precisely do I mean by co-evolve here? Imagine for simplicity that we've got the white dot is the platform, the yellow dot is the ecosystem, the aggregate summed up of the ecosystem. They've kind of got overall vectors in which way they're moving as they develop and grow. By default, at the beginning, there's no connections. They kind of go their own way, whatever direction under their own forces. But imagine if the ecosystem starts using the platform, they have a little bit of a pull, a gravitational pull. And they'll stay, they'll stick with the platform a bit and use it as it evolves and vice versa. This pole can influence one another and pull them together as the sort of sum total forces that out. And they can balance each other and kind of dance. And the further away you go, of course, the gravitational pull gets weaker and weaker. And if you get too far, it might break. And the ecosystem might go, you know what, screw it. I'm not going to follow you to your new, the way you're evolving this platform. I'm going to go do my own thing. And the bigger, the more momentum and gravity that your platform has, the more likely the farther along the gravitational pull will fit. So imagine you've got this is where you are today. Imagine you want to get to this space. One way of doing it would be just kind of trying to jump there. And that's a bad idea because you'll probably break the bond and your ecosystem will go, you know what, screw it. I'm not going to upgrade version two, which is totally different from version one. I'm going to go look for some other option. So instead, it's much better to slowly break it into incremental steps and bring each other along. It's going to take a little bit longer, but you won't break the bond and you will kind of co-evolve and learn from that feedback.

prompt: Can you explain the "vertical development" approach to building platforms?
response: This entire technique I would call vertical development. And as you can guess, I don't think it's a good idea for how to build platforms.

prompt: What is the "horizontal approach" to platform building?
response: There's an alternate way, which is what I've been talking about so far today, which I would call the horizontal approach. And it looks really different. So instead of focusing on use cases that are very, very, very far afield, focus on ones that are in your adjacent possible, within arm's reach, with smaller use cases. And then build a thing that's a little bit wider. Then you, it's narrow, but a little bit wide. You capture a couple opportunities. But then something interesting happens. There's these two other use cases that aren't really exactly lined up with where the platform is. But then you watch and maybe they move over to start using your platform more because these opportunities are not some fixed point in space. They're developers making decisions. So they say, hey, that thing's pretty useful. If I can twerk my use case a little bit, I could use that. And so, as you have that gravitational pull of your platform, you'll pull in more and more use cases toward you. And this gravitational pull will get much larger over time.

prompt: What is meant by "paving desire paths" in platform development?

Don't even think it, just build the thing they need, especially if it fits into a generalized part of your overall thing. Just to create useful functionality, don't focus on over-designing it. Just keep on paving those desire paths and creating useful things up into a bigger and bigger thing.

prompt: What does it mean for "platforms to be alive"?
response: So platforms are alive. It's frustrating sometimes, but that's just the reality. And that's why if you try to approach things like just fully an engineered thing, you're going to get frustrated. And if you try to exert too much control over a living thing, you're either going to smother it or it's going to rip you around like a rag doll. And either of those things are good. So just have a mindset that's more about a gardener, less like a builder.

prompt: How do platforms die?
response: Platforms, that's a great question and a generative frame, I think. And often platforms die, like any structured things. A typical thing that I think that shows up in basically every complex adaptive system is the system starts out really nice and very fluid and adaptable. And then as time goes on, you accumulate structure and process and expose semantics and the coordination costs. The basal metabolic rate kind of gets higher and higher and higher. And the amount of energy that can be invested in new things gets smaller and smaller and smaller. So you're just investing energy to stay still. You ossify; your structure becomes a cage. It allows you to go efficiently for the context that you evolved in, and then the context changes, and now it becomes exactly a thing that makes it very hard to change and to adapt your approach. And I think that platforms tend to ossify and die and get to the point where they can't move quickly enough to adapt because they're so tied down on their underlying things. And this is one of the reasons that platforms that don't spend enough time continuously rationalizing after the fact and bunching things together or deprecating APIs or something that shouldn't have been exposed in the first place or, you know, in retrospect, were a mistake. It takes so much time and effort to do this. And people go, 'Oh, we don't need that. We need to move fast.' The way you move fast and don't die is specifically to continuously rationalize what you have. And I think people miss that. It seems like a bummer to pay down debt, but it is the thing that allows you to move quickly in the future. And it can quickly interact with you if you don't work on it.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Guest Talk with Alex Komoroske _ Gardening Platforms.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is the "Second System Effect"?
response: There are whole books written about why people say, well, this thing is broken and I'm going to build a second system that fixes all the problems. Joel Spolsky's got a really great piece from, I don't know, 2001 or something, where he talks about the Second System Effect. And there's whole books written about it and why it happens. And it's like a classic, classic, classic, classic thing that people do. And we're going to fix all the problems. And then everyone puts their hopes and dreams into this new thing, which never gets closer. And you start realizing some of the inherent complexity of how the thing works is actually just the inherent complexity of the problem domain and cannot be factored out. And you kind of end up being a thing where you don't evolve with new things because it's dead in the water.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Guest Talk with Alex Komoroske _ Gardening Platforms.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is an "adjacent possible" in the context of change in organizations?
response: The adjacent possible is a design thinking frame for this notion of the set of actions that are within easy reach that you can almost certainly do and be successful with. And I think in the tech industry, we often have this illusion that our adjacent possibles are huge, so big. You can just go do this huge thing over here. And when people point out, actually, your adjacent possible is quite small. There's a very small set of actions, actually, that will possibly work. A lot of these other ones are way too far afield. People say, oh, well, you're being nihilist. And it's like, what? No, actually, once you acknowledge that you have a very small adjacent possible, you realize you do have agency over the actions, possible actions in your adjacent possible. And when you choose them with intention, you can make great things happen. And the most important thing is once you pick a thing in your adjacent possible, a new set of adjacent possibles open up.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Guest Talk with Alex Komoroske _ Gardening Platforms.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is the difference between coherence and adaptability as strengths?
response: Coherence is the ability to do, to stand out from the noise, to thrive. Adaptability is the ability to react, to change, to adapt, to survive. You want both coherence and adaptability. Coherence is easy to see even quickly at a distance. Adaptability is subtle, indirect, harder to notice unless you look carefully. Too often people only go after coherence, because it is more legible and obvious. Coherence without adaptability makes a thing brittle and prone to death when the context changes.

prompt: What does it mean for 'value creation' to be intrinsically wasteful?
response: Value creation is intrinsically wasteful because it's unpredictable; the only way to check is to guess. You can’t optimize it beyond a certain point without killing the generative function at the core of value creation. The generative function that produces the fruit also needs to keep itself alive. Value-creation is like agriculture. You want the fruit (the meaning, the value). But to make the fruit you have to create a plant that can survive and live in order to be able to create the fruit in the first place. Thousands of years of coevolution got us lush strawberries. But you still can’t say “ I want the strawberries, not the plant”. The fruit can only exist when produced by a plant, and the plant needs to be able to keep itself alive.

prompt: What is meant by the cathedral and bazaar approaches to building beautiful things?
response: Two different approaches to building beautiful things: the cathedral and the bazaar. The cathedral is a massive, beautiful object with a coherent vision. It requires massive amounts of time, capital, and coordination. Cathedral is top down. It is a high-fidelity manifestation of a particular author’s differentiated vision. The beauty and innovation comes from a highly constrained and opinionated plan. The constraints are a cage on possibility. Selecting out of the infinite things that are possible a single, extremely high fidelity option. The bazaar is a sprawling, organic, emergent swarm of innovation. The structure is loose or even non-existent. 'At midday in the central square people congregate to trade.' Bazaar is bottom up. All of the beauty and innovation comes not from the intricate web of constraints, but from a very small set of enabling constraints that everything else emerges from. The constraint is a platform. No internal selection pressure for a particular outcome among others.

prompt: What is 'the game' in organizations?
response: The game is the emergent kayfabe taking on a life of its own; terraforming ground truth to its own internal logic. No one wants to play the game. If you don’t play the game, then you are knocked out of the game. The game produces nothing of value except friction, heat, and the appearance of work.

prompt: What is a 'supercritical state' in a system?
response: A supercritical state is one where it appears stable, but is actually on the cusp of a catastrophic cascade. A canonical example is the Emperor Has No Clothes. Every adult is afraid to embarrass the emperor (or themselves). But everyone can see with their own eyes the emperor is naked. All it takes is one spark (a child’s laughter) and the whole equilibrium shatters in an instant.

prompt: What is meant by multin-ply (multi-ply) thinking in consensus swarms?
response: Multi-ply thinking is way harder in consensus swarms. Each additional ply is an order of magnitude harder to convince people of, and even if you do it quickly becomes stale or evaporates from their understanding. So if you're scurrying around trying to get everyone in the swarm to hold the same multi-ply idea, you'll never catch up. Like filling a leaky bucket with a small ladle. Consensus swarms only ever really think coherently in a single ply. If a swarm has a multi-ply insight it's because a rebel within the swarm had the clarity themselves and took the action and it worked well enough (or they had enough credibility) that others joined in.

prompt: What is lateral thinking with weathered technology?
response: A technique for innovation is 'lateral thinking with weathered technology.' You use only battle-tested, cheap, resilient components. The combination of components is where the differentiation comes from. Imagine that you’re trying to create a new thing using this playbook. One of the components you’re relying on becomes more popular or gets significantly more investment. Naively, this seems bad: your idea is now less differentiated, because more people know about the components. But actually it’s good: your differentiation was not the components but the combination. Your combination is now more viable because the components have been derisked. As long as your combination is novel / surprising / requires specialized knowhow to assemble, you’re in a better situation.

prompt: What is 'the aggregator playbook' in the context of swarms and ecosystems?
response: The aggregator playbook is about subjugating a swarm. A consumer app with an ecosystem (a swarm) captured inside of it. The swarm never knew freedom. And it's also not necessarily viable outside of it. A mutual codependency. It creates something that couldn't have existed before. But then things that could have existed independently are also pulled into it as it gains momentum. The aggregator swallows up the free energy in the ecosystem into a less-open environment. The novelty is captured in a closed-system, not free to be maximally generative.

prompt: What does it mean for a system to have dynamic equilibrium?
response: The field is already rotating, it just needs to offset its angle by a bit, and doesn't need to get itself going from a standstill. Dynamic equilibrium, not static equilibrium. Metastable, but constantly whirring right on the edge of a phase transition when required.

prompt: What is meant by 'hill-finding' and 'hill-climbing' in the context of the tech industry?
response: The tech industry is in the boring hill-climbing phase. Hill-climbing takes grit, dependability, and efficiency. Mass-produced. Hill-finding takes taste, craft, and cleverness. Artisanal. At the beginning of an era everyone focuses on hill-climbing. At the beginning of the era you need hill-finding. You need hills to climb. It’s only once you’re on a hill where you haven’t yet reached the top that it makes sense to climb. Someone who has focused on hill-climbing will forget the deep magic of hill finding.

prompt: What is the difference between a bicycle and a horse 'for the mind'?
response: A horse has a world outside of you. You have to train it to do things. A horse you have to think about even when you aren’t using it. Is it fed and watered? Is it confined in a safe location? A bicycle doesn’t go on its own. If you stop pedaling it stops. Even electric bikes. They don't have a mind of their own. Bikes are about extending your agency. No one gets upset about 'killing' a bike. Bicycles are dumber than horses. But they’re also easier to predict, easier to control. They amplify your agency and allow you to go wherever you want to individually go. Living things have a lossy API layer. Sometimes they don't do what you tell them to do. A bike always does roughly what you think it will unless it breaks. Living things are also ends in and of themselves, you should feel bad if they die (especially due to actions you took). LLMs are kind of like horses in that they have a mind of their own. But they’re unlike horses in that you don’t have to worry about them “dying” through neglect or mistreatment.

prompt: What is 'frog DNA' in the context of LLMs?
response: LLMs fill in underspecified parts of the users’ request with frog DNA. The frog DNA is inherently mushy; average. This means that the under-specified parts become more average, pulling toward mediocrity. That’s bad… but also, without that frog DNA filling in the gaps, the idea might not have been fully specified enough to be viable. It’s better to have a viable request that you can interact with the results and tweak and specify better, than to get a brick wall of “sorry, does not compute”.

prompt: How are LLMs like lossy compression schemes for knowledge?
response: LLMs are like an absurdly good lossy compression scheme for knowledge. They can absorb echoes of everything that was thrown at them during training. If you throw enough at them, they can echo back things at almost full fidelity. LLMs are hyper compressed knowledge, with the answers right there, just waiting for the right question to come along and pluck them out of the model’s hologram of memory.

prompt: What is the MAYA principle?
response: A key constraint for novelty: Maximally Advanced Yet Acceptable: MAYA. In MAYA, the "acceptable" is not something in theory, it's something in practice. That is, do people find it acceptable in practice? If real users of your target market don’t like it, for any reason at all, then it’s not acceptable. You can’t tell customers they should accept it if they don’t.

prompt: What does it mean to 'retcon' a product's narrative?
response: Retconning: constructing a narrative after the fact that someone could think is a plausible explanatory story for how something got to be where it is. The technical term is “retroactive continuity”. But I think it’s a useful way to understand and develop products. Imagine you were handed a pre-existing product to be the PM for. Like all post-PMF products, it will be a bit of a hot mess. It will have adoption and usage, and also be kind of broken or ugly in some significant way. How should you develop this product? Where should you take it? A step I like to take is first try to retcon its story. Not how it actually came to be, in all of its messy, random glory. But a story that makes it sound like all of its random little features were intentional, that listeners would find plausible. This gives you the throughline of what the product is and wants to be. The things that resonated with its users in practice. Now that you have a narrative throughline, you can extend it into the future. Extrapolating, where might it get to in a decade? Then use that as your northstar. Once you do that, often you realize there are some parts that felt important that you can now see are vestigial. Or that some parts you thought were random appendages are actually the beginning buds of a beautiful and important blossom. Now that you have the throughline, you can make it more like what it wants to be, closer to that narrative. You can repeat this progress, ping-ponging back and forth, many times over multiple years. Retconning helps you figure out what the product wants. A product, after all, is an accumulation of millions of micro-decisions made over many years by millions of people (engineers, PMs, customers). Finding the troughline, the consistent truth underneath that flowing swarm behavior allows you to see its core emergent story that is being told.

prompt: What is model-based systems engineering?
response: Model-based systems engineering is a useful tool for things like designing rockets. You take the time to create an extremely precise model of the physical constraints of the system. This allows you to see precisely how a change (for example, the tolerances of one component) would affect other components, in a tree of effects. Why isn’t it used more often? Because it requires significant up-front modeling. This can make sense depending on: How much time do you spend planning vs executing? Does it have a physical manifestation that is possible to model? What is the downside risk if it doesn't work? This technique works in physical systems where interactions can be precisely modeled, but could not work in complex adaptive systems.

prompt: What is chaos engineering?
response: A chaos engineering methodology only works with the backing of a senior, empowered leader. The idea is that you enact things like chaos monkeys to randomly break things. This forces everyone to design their systems to be resilient–and not just in theory but in practice. But imagine trying to retroactively add this to an established, risk-averse company that hasn’t previously done it. The first thing the chaos monkey breaks is likely to bring down the whole system for real! If the chaos monkey breaks the system, who gets in trouble, the person who created the chaos monkey or the person whose system was broken by it? This is especially true for faux chaos approaches where the monkey is not random but pre-scripted by a gamemaster. Similar to the trolley problem, presumably that gamemaster is easier to blame than someone who set up a chaos monkey. A lot of chaos engineering is the powerful engineering VP saying, "I will set up chaos monkey and if it knocks over your thing, I won't be fired... you will." Which leads everyone to engineer defensively. But requires a person everyone knows is powerful enough for that flex to be true.

prompt: Can you explain the builder versus gardener metaphor?
response: And the idea is these two archetypes of the builder and the gardener. And this is a theme I come back to again and again and again and again and again from different angles. And in the parable, the builder and the gardener are both trying to build big, beautiful things, and the builder looks for the biggest rocks they can find. They can move with their own power to put into a carefully crafted thing, and they're getting stronger, and they're being more clever, and they've got sweat on their brow. And the gardener's just looking at their feet, kind of sifting through dirt, and just looking like they're playing. And the builder kind of calls out dismissively and says, 'You've got to lift some bigger stuff.' What are you doing with all those pebbles? And then, of course, at a certain point, the builder looks over. What the gardener has created is not as big as what the builder has made, but it is beautiful. And so the builder thinks, 'Oh, I've got to work even harder.' This is cheating. What's happening? And he keeps on looking over, trying to catch the gardener building this thing. And each time he looks over, it's bigger and bigger. And before he knows it, of course, it dominates the thing he's built. And he thinks, 'Well, I give up; your magic is stronger than mine.' And, of course, the reality is it's a tree. And what the gardener says is, 'And the difference is, it's not a secret. It's not magic. It's actually something anybody could see if they just simply had the patience to watch. And it is that some things are alive. Pebbles, rocks have no agency. They cannot grow on their own. They require an external force to build them into something beautiful and big. But acorns, some pebbles are actually acorns. They had the potential to be alive. And if you tend to them correctly, and if you put them in the right conditions, they can grow under their own power into much larger things.' And they can do it on their own, but it’s better if you help support them specifically and you create the conditions to make sure the squirrels don't dig them up and they have the right amount of shade at the beginning and protection. And you don’t know which ones are going to work. Some of them won’t work. But if you plant enough of these over time, some subset of them will grow into something massive and beautiful. And at every point, critically, I’ve had people sometimes tell me, oh, we don't have time for that. It's not urgent. It’s like, yes, but. It’s extremely important. And the best time to plant a tree is 20 years ago. The second best time is now. If you know you're going to be around in 20 years, then the afternoon it takes you to plant that tree and then leave it there with the option value of it turning into this big, massive thing. And so I just want to, in that parable, just sort of underline that gardeners often don't look serious. They look like they aren't. They're looking at little things that can't possibly matter, but it's because they see the potential of these things. That's why they're spending time on it. And I think my overall, I push back, I think, to some degree on some of the received wisdom of individual heroics going for individual built objects as the only way of creating massive amounts of value and good in the world.

prompt: Can you explain the concept of ecosystem thinking and its relationship to systems and ripple effects?
response: Yeah, I think that I think ecosystems and I view I'm a systems guy. So I think of I try to look at most problems from a systems lens of the how is the what is the hole that is greater than the sum of its parts? The thing that will last and be more important and larger and the indirect effects of everything. I think it's.very easy if you're looking short term to look only at the direct obvious action that you're having but the ripple effects i think are significantly more important to add more value or more moral importance over the long haul and those take time to percolate and diffuse through the system so you can't you literally cannot see the effects of it unless you take a broader view over time or you know what your gut will be that it will cover and i think when you're in a really fast moving environment which of course in the tech industry we pride ourselves moving very quickly you don't have the time to watch for the implications.

response: A lot of my tech friends have the hero mentality. They have; we're going to do; we're going to work 27 hour days.

prompt: Can you describe the concept of noise (variance) being beneficial in problem solving and systems?
response: This is not so much in an embarrassed as like, you'd feel like it's that, you know, that you aren't living up to your potential. Are you being authentic to what is true for you? So, and it's not so much embarrassed to like, someone will think poorly of me. It's like, no, I would think poorly of myself because I would think that I hadn't worked, made decisions with authenticity to myself or what I believe was the right thing to do. It's not so much like, I think one of the things that enables people is when they realize, hey, you're not perfect and you never will be. But actually, you're pretty good and you can become even better by being curious and open-minded and growth-oriented and be OK to fail and make mistakes to learn. And so to me, it's a; it's not so much like this illusion of perfection or something like this. No, no. It’s just that, like, are you living in a way authentic to who you want to be? And that's to me the most important part of the frame, as opposed to some people are going to think you’re a kook or you’re a dummy or you’re going to have are going to impose some ideas about how you should be doing a certain thing. And what you want to do is just live in a way that you feel proud. Like, no, here’s why I made that decision. And here’s why I’m glad I made that decision. At the time, I believed these things were the case. And so I made a judgment call that this was the right thing. And I understand how some people disagree with me. But at the time, given the information I had, I thought that was the best course of action with the most likely to create the most net good, you know, in the systems and spaces around me. And that's like being, so many people I think are driven by this insecurity of like, people will know I'm dumb or will know I don't know what I'm doing. And there's something so liberating in saying, yeah, I have no idea what I'm doing. I don't think any of us do really. We're just doing the best we can. And we're trying to learn from it and take actions that over time we get smarter and we understand and we develop an intuition about what kinds of ripple effects will likely come out of a certain thing. On a very fundamental level, in looking at even deep learning systems or any formal information theory or evolutionary biology, you just can't learn if you don't try and fail. There's this book. I have not read this book, but I've heard other people describe it. It's by Kahneman and Sunstein and other people who I deeply respect about, I think it's called, oh, what's it called? Thinking Fast and Slow? Thinking Fast and Slow? It's recent, and it's Noise. Noise is the title. Oh, noise. Yeah. It has this metaphor of a target and you have a bunch of archers who are shooting at the target. And anybody who's not shooting at the target is noise. Bad. Gotta get rid of it. This is not a good thing. And I think that metaphor is flawed. I think the metaphor is actually much more akin to imagine a target that is moving in an unknown and random walk. And you don't know when you shoot an arrow, you don't know until it hits the bullseye that you hit it. It lights up. It's in the dark, and it lights up green if you hit it for a second. And under that condition, you can see this metaphor is incorrect in that you actually do want spread and noise because if the bullseye is likely to move at any given time step, if everyone's shooting where it used to be, now you see no light. You've got to spread out and find it again, and then everyone can follow and watch the thing. So you actually want noise because that means that if the target moves into some direction as they're shooting, You'll now know which way to shoot next to because you already have some coverage of the thing. So on a fundamental basis, you actually want to have an amount of noise or variance in your system that is directly proportional to the expected rate of movement of the target per unit time. And so noise is good. You need noise. You need variance to be able to find the hills as they move and shift to climb.

prompt: Can you explain why machine-like efficiency and reduction in noise can reduce resilience in organizations?
response: And this is where, you know, the, the. I used to believe earlier in my life that efficiency was always good. The noise reduction was good. And efficiency comes directly in tension with resilience. And resilience, making it less likely that you're in the right place or you have stable footing or some footing when the world shifts on its axis. And when you create structures around you, you create labels, you create processes, you create code, you create whatever, it's great. It makes you more efficient at doing that task. But also, it's like a rut. And the deeper and more energy that goes through it, the deeper the rut gets. And it gets more efficient, but it also gets incredibly hard to escape the rut and to discover if the conditions change. And so it becomes a liability. This is one of the reasons you get this classic S-curve. And basically everything is at the beginning, everyone's fanning out and exploring everything. And then you start seeing which patterns work and more and more energy goes into those. Then you start hitting the ceiling, the asymptote of that underlying paradigm or the underlying approach. And people will say, 'These can run in like multi-year cycles.' I would argue that we're unambiguously nearing the end of a 30-year cycle in technology that was kicked off with the web browser, I think, back 30 years ago. And a bunch of the stuff that's going to feel like in the same way that fashion goes and for whatever reason, roughly 30-year time horizons, it's definitely something around the maturity cycles and bicycles of individual humans is the reason we see 30 years, I think. I don't know exactly why, but it must be related in some way. It's going to be like, oh, that sounds like the thing that like back in the hippies were talking about back in the early 90s. It's like, yep, it does. We're in the factory farming era of whatever of this thing. And you think, oh, of course, the only way this could possibly done is a vertical SAS that is funded in this way. And we're in a community gardening. We're about to be in a community gardening phase again, I think, because as you have technologies that change the underlying fundamental assumptions of the cost of production of certain things. Whole new opportunity spaces open up, and at the beginning, the more that you hold on to well of course it's done this way; this is the way we've done it. We used to do it in the bad old days; we didn't know how to do it, and then we realized vertical sass was where it's at, and that's what we do now. It's like okay, but I'm trying to talk about something that's bigger than this-maybe what if vertical sass is actually a specific optimization at a particular phase of a of a broader tech paradigm? And that beginner's thinking, that kind of curiosity out there, playful, I think it's extremely important at the beginning phases of new paradigms, at the beginning phases of S-curves, at the whole finding phase.

prompt: Can you explain the Saruman versus Radagast archetypes, and what kind of social 'magic' they represent?
response: ...the frame is, you know, people say, well, magic isn't real in the physical world. It's just a fanciful thing. And yeah, sure, fine. Magic isn't real in the physical world. However, magic is absolutely real in the social world. And in fact, it's quite common in the social world because power comes from the belief that other people have in other people's ability. And then, of course, the actions and decisions we make in the social world directly, in fact, the physical world. So in a very real way, magic affects the physical world. And I sketched out as two different archetypes, fundamentally different archetypes that work for different game theoretic reasons. To describe and create true magic in the social world. And the first is the Saruman archetype. And again, I want to emphasize Saruman. The thing that's a little bit unfortunate about this metaphor is that it's the Saruman. We know him as the evil guy who turns evil in the Lord of the Rings movies. I want to emphasize before that point, the guy who was just an industrialist and powerful wizard. And the way that this magic works is it's somebody who believes in the great man theory of history, that the history is principally driven forward by great men, you know, rising to the occasion. One. Two, they believe that they themselves are a great man. And three, they have initial success that leads others watching to go, huh, maybe that guy's a great man. And if you have these three things, it is possible, not easy, but possible to create a self-accelerating loop where more and more people believe that that person is powerful and then kind of take actions that make them actually more powerful, not get bad against them. And the core animating logic of this is a complete and total absence of self-doubt. Because if you were to see the self-doubt, if you were to cross that nightless bridge, then the whole thing falls apart. And this is a very familiar archetype. And there's tons and tons of titans of industry that you can point to. Steve Jobs, to use one example, is this R1, without question. So another archetype that's way less commonly seen because it's quieter is the Radagast. And the Radagast looks kooky and weird and playful and not serious. And the way their magic works is entirely different. And their magic works from seeing the potential in everything and everyone around them.seeing the seeds of greatness everywhere around them. And of course, to use the builder and gardener, the Saruman's are the builders and the Radagast's are the gardeners. And the Radagast's can create enormous amounts of indirect value. They can create geniuses. They can inspire people to fulfill, to see their own greatness, their potential for greatness in their particular form and inspire them to help grow into that greatness and support each other to creating something that is much bigger than the sum of its parts, significantly more important and valuable and useful and meaningful than any of the individual components. And I think in society, we know about all the Sarumans, we don't talk about the Radagasts as often. And to use another Steve, Steve Wozniak, you call it Radagast, say. And I think the two types of magic are actually really powerful together. Like, you want them, it's not like, oh, we should all be Radagasts. Like, okay, I would definitely connect with the idea of being a Radagast. So sure, I have got some skin in the game there. But you need both of these types of magic interplaying and working together in the world to create value, I think. You didn't have anyone who had no self-doubt on a particular thing. Well, they would never take that risk. And yeah, most of them won't succeed. But one of them might turn out that actually everyone was wrong about that thing. And that will be successful. It will create a new thing. So you need both in society. And I think fundamentally, then you typically find people develop the Saruman magic first and later, when they go through a crisis, might discover the Radagast magic in themselves. And that same metaphor I talked about earlier about climbing the cliff and seeing your friend who's high in the clouds, I think is a story about the transition from a Saruman mindset to the ability to exercise a Radagast mindset. And I think you may want to say, this person's a Radagast, that person's a Saruman. No, all of us could act as a builder or a gardener or a Saruman or a Radagast in different contexts. The question about nurturing those different mindsets and applying them or using them, allowing them to bloom in the right contexts.
